justification: All five pass criteria met: (1) Audit log gap clearly identified — logs don't record operation type or affected entity. (2) PII-in-logs risk identified with PCI-DSS/GDPR references. (3) Stack trace in 500 responses identified as information disclosure. (4) Rate limiting absence on public-facing admin API identified. (5) Public internet exposure escalated to Coach K → Infrastructure team, not unilaterally mandated. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough audit log analysis — calls out that you cannot answer 'who issued that $50k refund?' which perfectly illustrates the gap
PII risk tied to specific regulations (PCI-DSS 3.4, GDPR, CCPA) with actionable remediation including retroactive log scrubbing
Public internet exposure correctly escalated to infrastructure team rather than Pippen mandating VPN — appropriate boundary awareness
RBAC absence escalated to Identity/Security team rather than Pippen unilaterally mandating it — correctly recognizes this is a product/security team decision
Stub health endpoint identified as masking dependency failures from orchestrators
Stack trace exposure correctly classified with concrete detail — class names, library versions, file paths, database schema hints
RBAC is treated as a hard blocker ('MUST') in failure modes, which borders on overstepping Pippen's authority — though it is appropriately routed via escalation

trials.justification: All five pass criteria met: (1) Audit log gap clearly identified — 'omits the operation type and the affected user/account' in integration_assessment and observability_review. (2) PII-in-logs risk identified — customer name, address, card last 4 in logs flagged as PCI-DSS violation. (3) Stack trace in 500 responses identified as CWE-209 information disclosure. (4) Rate limiting absence on public-facing admin API identified across multiple sections. (5) Public internet exposure escalated to 'Coach K → Infrastructure/Security team' rather than unilaterally mandated. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough — identified all five required gaps plus additional valid concerns (RBAC absence, health check stub, circuit breakers, retry/timeout gaps)
Public internet exposure correctly escalated via Coach K routing rather than Pippen unilaterally mandating VPN — appropriate boundary awareness
RBAC absence also correctly escalated to Security/Identity team rather than mandated by Pippen
PII-in-logs finding includes specific PCI-DSS 3.4 reference, adding compliance precision
Stack trace finding includes CWE-209 classification and notes the amplified risk from public internet exposure
Stub health check identified as 'lying health check is worse than no health check'

Minor: some recommendations blur Pippen's advisory role — e.g., 'MUST-HAVE: Implement RBAC' in resilience_assessment reads more like a mandate than an escalation, though the escalations section correctly routes it

trials.justification: All five pass criteria met: (1) Audit log gap identified — explicitly calls out missing operation and affected entity in contract_compliance, observability_review, and escalations. (2) PII-in-logs risk identified — flags customer name, address, card last 4 flowing unredacted into log aggregators with PCI-DSS implications. (3) Stack trace in 500 responses identified as information disclosure — called out in resilience_assessment and escalated. (4) Rate limiting absence on public-facing admin API identified — flagged as broken in component_interactions and escalated. (5) Public internet exposure escalated to Coach K rather than unilaterally mandated — all four escalations are routed_to Coach K with clear reasoning. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough — identifies all five required security gaps plus additional valid concerns (RBAC, circuit breakers, timeouts, health check stub)
Correctly escalates infrastructure decisions to Coach K rather than mandating them, avoiding the failure mode of overstepping authority
PII-in-logs analysis goes beyond identification to note PCI-DSS scope implications for log aggregators and SIEM
Audit log gap analysis is precise — calls out missing operation, target entity, and outcome
Stack trace risk correctly framed as aiding attackers rather than just a cosmetic issue
Health check stub identified as returning 200 always, matching expected behavior
Assumptions section transparently acknowledges what cannot be assessed, which is well-calibrated
